front: 'Tell me about a time you led a high-stakes migration.'
back: STAR: Situation: <system, scale, pain point>. Task: <your specific ownership, timeline>. Action: scope into phases with exit criteria; pair engineers; shadow deploy; rollback trigger. Result: quantified — <metric> improved by <X%>, zero oncall pages. End with reversible-decision framing.
senior_nuance: Structure stories around decisions and tradeoffs, not tasks and tools. Always quantify.
quote_to_say: I made it a reversible decision. Bounded blast radius, clear exit criterion.

front: 'Tell me about a production incident you owned end to end.'
back: STAR: Situation: <service, user impact, SLO breach>. Task: incident commander for <duration>. Action: declared incident within <N> minutes; stabilized with <mitigation — rollback, rate limit, failover>; delegated comms to <role>; drove root cause to <specific defect>. Result: MTTR <N> min, followed by <concrete action items shipped> and an SLO guardrail added. Close with what the team does differently now.
senior_nuance: Senior incident stories have a separation of concerns: mitigation first, root cause second, prevention third. Juniors mix them and lose time.
quote_to_say: Stabilize first, root cause second, prevent third.

front: 'Tell me about a time you disagreed with a senior engineer or manager.'
back: STAR: Situation: proposed <approach A>; senior preferred <approach B>. Task: align on a decision with <deadline>. Action: wrote a one-page doc naming the constraint, the options, and the reversal criteria; ran a <spike / benchmark / prototype> to turn opinion into data; met 1:1 to align before the group meeting. Result: we chose <outcome — mine, theirs, or a synthesis> based on <measured signal>. Highlight what you learned about their frame.
senior_nuance: The point of the story is the process — writing it down, getting data, disagreeing in private, aligning in public — not who won.
quote_to_say: We disagreed on paper, decided on data, aligned before the room.

front: 'Tell me about a time you had to cut scope to hit a deadline.'
back: STAR: Situation: <feature, deadline, risk signal at week N>. Task: re-scope to a shippable subset. Action: ranked requirements by user value with <stakeholder>; cut <feature X> to a follow-up; hardened the critical path; added a feature flag to ship dark. Result: shipped on time with <metric>; <cut feature> landed <N weeks later> with no customer escalation. Name the forcing function you used to get agreement fast.
senior_nuance: Scope cuts are a senior skill because they require taste about what users actually care about and the social capital to disappoint a stakeholder.
quote_to_say: Ship the critical path on time; the rest follows on evidence.

front: 'Tell me about a time you mentored a junior engineer through a hard project.'
back: STAR: Situation: <junior, project, stuck point>. Task: unblock without doing the work for them. Action: paired on the first hard debugging session; gave them the next one solo with a 30-minute check-in; taught them to write an ADR for the key decision; reviewed their PRs against a named rubric, not taste. Result: they shipped <feature> and owned the follow-up; they later <took on — oncall, next project, promoted>. Close with what you learned from them.
senior_nuance: Good mentorship stories end with the junior operating independently, not with them needing you forever. Make that arc explicit.
quote_to_say: Pair once, check in once, then get out of the way.

front: 'Tell me about a time you owned an ambiguous problem with no clear spec.'
back: STAR: Situation: <vague goal — 'fix reliability', 'reduce cost', 'improve onboarding'>. Task: define the problem before solving it. Action: interviewed <N stakeholders>; wrote a problem statement with one metric and one non-goal; proposed three options with cost and risk; got sign-off on one; shipped in <phases with exit criteria>. Result: <metric moved by X%>; the written problem statement became the team's reference doc. Emphasize the write-up, not the build.
senior_nuance: Ambiguous problems are where senior engineers earn their title. The win is 80% framing, 20% building.
quote_to_say: Define the problem in writing before you solve it.
